coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot-induced oxygen vacancies in copper vanadate enabling persulfate photoactivation for tetracycline degradation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Ernan
Li, Shijia
Wu, Jie
Chang, Qing
Li, Ning
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description:
Synchronously creating oxygen vacancies (OVs) and an internal electric field (IEF) in photocatalysts could be an ideal strategy to facilitate photogenerated charge separation and surface reactions but remain unexplored for this use. In this work, we report that low-cost and multifunctional CDs can involve in the nucleation reaction of copper vanadates (CuVs) to create OVs and proper IEF at the interface by modulating the valence states of coppers under hydrothermal conditions. Thus, CDs synergistically serve as oxygen vacancy inducer and charge separator in CuVs to extract photogenerated carriers to trigger persulfate (PS) activation for the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC). It turns out that CDs-modulated CuVs exhibit the expected photocatalytic capacity to activate PS in water and enable TC decomposition efficiency approximately 8 times higher than CDs-free CuVs under visible light irradiation. Our investigations elucidate that the oxidative breakdown of TC is dominated by the active species cooperation of 1O2 with h+ and
OH formed in photocatalytic reaction system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper vanadate
Oxygen vacancy
Photocatalysis
Persulfate activation
Carbon dots
Pollution degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the formation processes of CDs@CuVs; (b) XRD patterns of CuVs and CDs@CuVs; (c and d) TEM and HRTEM images of CDs@CuVs; (e) FT-IR spectra of CuVs, NH4VO3-treated CDs and CDs@CuVs; (f) The elemental mapping images of Cu, V, O, C, and N elements for CDs@CuVs.

XPS (a) Cu 2p and (b) O 1 s spectra of CuVs and CDs@CuVs; (c) EPR results of CuVs and CDs@CuVs; (d) Photocurrent responses; (e) Electrochemical impedance plots and (f) Fluorescence lifetime of CuVs, NH4VO3-treated CDs and CDs@CuVs;

(a) The photocatalytic degradation of TC for CuVs and CDs@CuVs; (b) the corresponding pseudo first order kinetic curves; (c-f) Effects of various anions on TC removal including Cl− (c), SO4 2− (d), NO3 − (e) and HCO3 − (f).

(a) Cycling stability of CDs@CuVs for TC degradation; (b) XRD patterns of the fresh and the used CDs@CuVs; (c) XPS Cu 2p spectra of the fresh and the used CDs@CuVs; (d) EPR results of the fresh and the used CDs@CuVs.
(a) Effect of different scavengers on TC degradation; (b-d) Spin-trapping EPR spectra for OH (b), O2 ▪− (c) and (d) 1O2.
(a) Illustration of possible catalytic mechanisms; (b) The amperometric i-t curve of CDs@CuVs in response to PS and TC addition, respctively; (c) In-situ FT-IR spectra of CDs@CuVs.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dot-induced oxygen vacancies in copper vanadate enabling persulfate photoactivation for tetracycline degradation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Coal and CBM Co-Mining

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Coal and CBM Co-Mining, Jincheng 048012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Synchronously creating oxygen vacancies (OVs) and an internal electric field (IEF) in photocatalysts could be an ideal strategy to facilitate photogenerated charge separation and surface reactions but remain unexplored for this use. In this work, we report that low-cost and multifunctional CDs can involve in the nucleation reaction of copper vanadates (CuVs) to create OVs and proper IEF at the interface by modulating the valence states of coppers under hydrothermal conditions. Thus, CDs synergistically serve as oxygen vacancy inducer and charge separator in CuVs to extract photogenerated carriers to trigger persulfate (PS) activation for the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC). It turns out that CDs-modulated CuVs exhibit the expected photocatalytic capacity to activate PS in water and enable TC decomposition efficiency approximately 8 times higher than CDs-free CuVs under visible light irradiation. Our investigations elucidate that the oxidative breakdown of TC is dominated by the active species cooperation of 1O2 with h+ and
OH formed in photocatalytic reaction system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-driven chemical decomposition and transformation promises a bright future in the remediation of emerging anthropogenic organic contaminants in surface water [1–3]. This technique is often performed by inexpensive and green photocatalysts that can harvest sunlight to create energetic carriers used to initiate decomposition reaction of organic pollutants [4–6]. However, limited by severe energy loss in the subsequent processes of photon absorption, charge separation/transfer and surface reactions upon inorganic semiconductors, their removal efficiency of organic pollutions usually falls far behind expectations. Creating oxygen vacancies (OVs) in metal oxide semiconductors is an efficient strategy to improve photocatalytic performance [5,7,8]. The roles of OVs played in catalytic reaction include: 1) regulating the electronic structure and charge mobility in catalysts; 2) facilitating the photogenerated charge separation and transfer; 3) optimizing the adsorption energy of reactants; 4) reducing the reaction energy barrier for molecular activation [8,9]. Many approaches such as thermal treatment, atomic substitution and chemical etching, have been used to design and construct of Ov, but they usually suffer from long reaction time, complicated and uncontrollable process as well as high manufacturing cost [10–12].

Carbon dots (CDs) possess numerous amazing features including rich functional groups, strong metal coordination, excellent electron donor/acceptor property, tunable photoluminescence and high conductivity [13–17]. In combination with their low cost, high chemical stability and environmental friendliness, CDs are therefore emerging as great nanocatalytic platforms to fulfill the sustainability needs of environmental remediation and chemical production [18]. Beyond the enhanced catalytic performance, the interest in CDs-based photocatalysts arises from the multiple functions of CDs in nanostructure synthesis, component control and surface activity regulation. This is due to the presence of reactive surface groups on CDs, which can directly participate in the formation of other components by means of redox reaction [19,20]. In order to design novel nanocomposites, for instance, CDs have been implemented to tune the valence states of coppers and their ratio in copper oxides under hydrothermal conditions [21]. Unique layered structure and multiple oxidation states render vanadates designable in crystal structure, morphology, energy band positions, optical and electrical properties [22,23]. Unfortunately, their photocatalytic performances are also hindered by the high recombination rate of carriers in bulk phase and on the surface. Constructing an appropriate internal electric field (IEF) with heterogeneous interfaces in metal vanadates is a popular strategy for charge separation [24–26], but simultaneously coupling IEF and surface OVs to separate and transfer charges for photocatalysis is rarely reported till now.

By activation, the persulfates (PS) can exhibit excellent oxidation capacity by generating a variety of highly active species including SO4
−,
OH, 1O2, and O2
−, which is more suitable for emerging contaminant degradation of antibiotics, endocrine disrupting chemicals and microplastics in surface water [27,28]. Among the reported activation technologies, solar-driven activation approach based on photocatalysis is an ideal option due to its satisfactory economic benefits, environmental friendliness, outstanding activation capacity and sustainability [29]. The chemical interaction of PS with pollutants is often slow and inefficient without solar irradiation [30]. Upon illumination, the photogenerated carriers are able to break the peroxide bonds in PS to release SO4
− radicals and then give rise to
OH formation through further reacting with OH− [31]. Triggering PS activation upon light usually requires the supports of transition metal ions, e.g. Cu2+, Fe2+, Ce3+, Co2+ via one electron transfer process [32]. The photoexcited carriers are transferred from the metal active sites to the adsorbed PS for the generation of active species to degrade pollutants [33]. Meanwhile, the delivered carriers from photocatalysts can directly involve redox reaction to generate
OH, 1O2, and O2
− for pollutant degradation [34]. Currently, the efficiency of PS activation relies on energy band structures and charge separation properties of the photocatalysts to a large extent. Hence, developing novel photocatalysts remains a feasible scheme for activating PS.

In this work, we make the first demonstration that CDs can involve in the nucleation reaction of copper vanadates (CuVs) to create OVs by modulating the valence states of coppers under hydrothermal conditions. Thus, CDs synergistically serve as oxygen vacancy inducer and charge separator in CuVs to extract photogenerated carriers to activate PS for the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), which is one of typical antibiotic contaminants in water bodies. The reactive site and the catalytic mechanism of CDs@CuVs irradiated by visible light were also clarified. Our work therefore opens up an avenue for photocatalyst design and PS activation.

The chemicals and reagents including copper chloride anhydrous (CuCl2, 98 %), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3, AR), triethanolamine (TEOA, AR), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC, USP), p-benzoquinone (BQ, 97 %), tert-butanol (TBA, 99.0 %), furfuryl alcohol (FFA, 98 %), formic acid (88 v/v%), sodium persulfate (PS, AR), concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, ∼12 M), and ammonia (NH3·H2O, AR) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.. Sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4, AR), methanol (MeOH, for HPLC), 2,2–6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, 97 %), 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolin N-oxygen (DMPO, 97 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Relevant information of other reagents was presented in Table S1. All chemicals were directly used as was and deionized water was used as the base solvent.

CDs were obtained by our reported method of selectively oxidizing coal pitch with mixed formic acid and hydrogen peroxide [35]. The obtained powders of CDs were washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times and then dispersed into deionized water by ultrasound to achieve the solution of CDs with the given concentration. First of all, 117 mg of NH4VO3 was added into 40 mL of deionized water and made it dissolve at 70 ℃ for achieving the homogeneous solution. Second, the obtained NH4VO3 solution mixed with 20 mL solution of CDs (2.7 g·L−1), subsequently transferring into a Teflon lined reaction vessel for further hydrothermal reaction. After reacting at 120 ℃ for 12 h, the brown solution was obtained. Third, taking 60 mL of CuCl2 solution with a concentration of 1.12 g·L−1 was added into the above acquired brown solution. After pH value adjusted to 9.0 with NH3·H2O, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon lined reaction vessel and then heated at 120 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling down the room temperature, finally, the precipitates were collected by centrifugation to achieve CDs@CuVs. The control sample of CuVs was also prepared by the reaction of CuCl2 with NH4VO3 under same hydrothermal conditions except for no addition of CDs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE powder diffractometer at Cu Kα with a scanning rate of 2 °min−1 in the 2θ range of 5–90°. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired using a Hitachi S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) were performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of all samples were acquired by a Kratos AXIS 165 mutitechnique electron spectrometer with an Al Ka X-ray source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by a Bruker Vertex 70 FTIR with a range of 400–4000 cm−1. Low-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were acquired on a Bruker EMXPLUS10/12 spectrometer (140 K, 9.062 GHz).

Typically, 5 mg of catalyst was added into 50 mL of TC solution (30 mg·L−1) and constantly stirred for 30 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, 150 μL (100 mM) of PS solution was added immediately, and the reaction was irradiated with a xenon lamp source (100 mW·cm−2) that had filtered out λ < 420 nm light. At a given interval, the 0.5 mL solution was taken out, filtered with 0.22 µm membrane, and mixed with 0.5 mL of methanol solution. The concentration of TC was determined using a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Essential LC-16, Japan) with a UV detector set at 356 nm, where C18 column was used to separate the organics. The mobile phase with a flow rate of 1.5 mL·min−1 was made of 25 % CH3OH and 75 % water along with 0.2 % formic acid. Every catalytic test was repeated for three times and the relative standard errors were less than 5 %.

In the quenching experiment, TEOA (1 mM), MeOH (200 mM), TBA (200 mM), BQ (1 mM) and FFA (1 mM) were added to the photoccatalytic reaction system as scavengers of h+, SO4
−, OH,
O
2
∙ -
and 1O2 radicals, respectively. The trapping experiments were conducted under the same reaction conditions and the initial pH of the photocatalytic system was around 7 without deliberate adjustment. The TC concentration in the solution with time was recorded by high performance liquid chromatography.

The catalyst in the aqueous solution was recovered by vacuum filtration. The solid was washed with water and ethanol alternately for 3 times, and then the filter cake was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 ℃. The dried cake was ground to perform the next degradation experiment.

Unlike with previously reported methods [36,37], in which CDs were directly added into the synthetic system, here we fabricated CDs@CuVs by two-step hydrothermal reactions, i.e. the dispersed CDs were first treated with NH4VO3 and subsequently reacted with CuCl2 under hydrothermal conditions of 120 ℃ (Fig. 1 a). Notably, ammonium ions of NH4VO3 can modulate the surface characteristics of CDs for controlling their interaction with the surrounding environment without the burden of introducing other impurities [38]. For comparison, CDs-free CuVs were prepared by the reaction of CuCl2 with NH4VO3 under hydrothermal conditions (Figure S1). The optical photographs of the powders of CDs@CuVs, CuVs,CDs, and NH4VO3-treated CDs show color changes caused by different compositions and structures (Figure S2). The XRD patterns of as-prepared samples display characteristic diffraction peaks at 13.0°, 29.8°, 35.2°, 37.5° and 61.3° that are assigned to the (201), (001), (223), (202), and (302) crystal planes of Cu3(VO4)2·3H2O (JCPDS 12–0523) respectively [39], indicating the existence of CuVs in the as-prepared CDs@CuVs and CuVs (Fig. 1b). Meanwhile, we estimated the average crystallite grain sizes of the samples by the Scherrer’s formula based on XRD results. The grain size of CDs@CuVs is 15.6 nm, smaller than that of CuVs (35.1 nm). The morphology and structure of as-prepared samples were characterized by SEM and TEM. Different from regularly stacked sheet-like structure and morphology of pure CuVs (Figure S3 and S4), the morphology of CDs@CuVs exhibits the mixed features consisting of sheet-like shapes and particle-like aggregation (Fig. 1c and S5), reflecting the effects of CDs-induced nucleation reaction. Moreover, HRTEM images of CDs@CuVs reveal that two kinds of crystal structures with the lattice fringes of 0.21 and 0.51 nm, which can be assigned to the (101) plane of graphite carbon (CDs) and the (201) lattice plane of Cu3(VO4)2·3H2O, respectively [40] (Fig. 1d). FT-IR spectra reflected the presence of CDs in the CDs@CuVs (Fig. 1e). From the FT-IR spectra of CDs@CuVs and NH4VO3-treated CDs, one can simultaneously observe the bonds at 1600–1700 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 that are attributed to CO/CC stretching vibration and NO stretching vibration, respectively. But these characteristic peaks are absent in the FT-IR spectrum of CuVs. The bonds at 537 cm−1 and 900–760 cm−1 are attributed to Cu–O stretching vibration, the symmetric and asymmetric tensile vibration of VO3 −, respectively [41]. The energy-dispersive (EDS) element mapping images present that the elements of Cu, V, O, C and N are evenly distributed throughout the CDs@CuVs, confirming the successful combination of CDs with CuVs as well (Fig. 1f). Moreover, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms confirm that CDs@CuVs have the larger specific area and richer pore structures than CuVs (Figure S6), thereby to the benefit of superior catalytic activities.

XPS was used to identify the chemical structures of different elements in samples (Figure S7 and Fig. 2 a, b). From XPS Cu 2p spectra in Fig. 2a, monovalent copper (Cu+) and divalent copper (Cu2+) coexist in both samples, but the ratio of Cu+/Cu2+ in CDs@CuVs is significantly higher than that of CuVs. It can be deduced that CDs act as a role of reducer in the formation of CuVs. The corresponding XPS O 1 s spectra were divided into three peaks at 529.9, 531.7 and 532.8 eV, which belong to the lattice oxygen (OL), OVs and adsorbed water molecules (OA), respectively [42] (Fig. 2b). Based on the peak ratio of OVs/OL, CDs@CuVs had the higher content of OVs than CDs-free CuVs. This result was confirmed by EPR. A strongly symmetric signal with a g-value of 2.003 was detected in CDs@CuVs, whereas this signal became very weak in pure CuVs owing to the capture of electrons by the reduced number of OVs (Fig. 2c). Consequently, CDs could induce local electron environment during the formation of CuVs, resulting in more OVs in CuVs. Benefiting from the introduction of CDs and OVs in CuVs, a new absorption feature appears at around 600 nm that extends to lower energy than CuVs, which indicates the presence of intermediate bands induced by OVs at the heterogeneous interfaces of CDs@CuVs (Figure S8). Meanwhile, the different band structures at the interface often result in an IEF to facilitate the carrier separation and transfer. To certify it, we measured the photocurrent responses of CuVs, NH4VO3-treated CDs and CDs@CuVs (Fig. 2d). It is clear that the current density of CDs@CuVs is significantly larger than that of CuVs and NH4VO3-treated CDs, thereby revealing the enhanced separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) show that the charge migration resistance for CDs@CuVs is much smaller than that of CuVs and NH4VO3-treated CDs owing to the effect of IEF (Fig. 2e). Therefore, the photoinduced charges from CDs@CuVs are more readily transferred to the catalyst surface for fulfilling surface reactions. Fig. 2f and Table S2 display the photoluminescence (PL) decay spectra of CuVs, NH4VO3-treated CDs and CDs@CuVs and their lifetime of the carriers. Since CDs can serve as electron reservoirs [15], NH4VO3-treated CDs possess a long PL lifetime as expected. After the integration of CDs into CuVs, the lifetime of carriers prolongs 1.65 times than that CuVs. The longer lifetime indicates that the carriers are endowed with more chances to migrate the surface, lessening the charge recombination ratio.

It is noted that TC in aqueous solution is very stable under visible light irradiation and is hardly decomposed even the presence of PS (Fig. 3 a). The catalytic activities of as-prepared samples toward TC degradation were examined under different conditions. Less than 18 % TC was consumed in the presence of the CDs@CuVs alone after the reaction for 50 min under visible light irradiation. After adding PS in the catalytic system, however, CDs@CuVs exhibited such a superior activity that nearly 100 % of TC was degraded without changing other conditions. These results proved that the TC degradation was realized by triggering PS with as-prepared photocatalysts. The similar cases also happened on CDs-free CuVs, but their phtotoactivities for TC removal lagged far behind those of CDs@CuVs. Fig. 3b depicts the comparisons of TC removal efficiencies under various reaction conditions. Degradation rate constant of CDs@CuVs reaches nearly 8 times higher than that of CuVs under PS activation. It turns out that the optimal CDs@CuVs exhibits the excellent photocatalytic capacity to activate PS in water. A series of control experiments were carried out to further make the catalytic process clear. First of all, we examined the effect of the added amount of CDs on the photocatalytic performance of CDs@CuVs (Figure S9). The proper amount of CDs fulfilled the ideal photocatalytic activity to degrade TC in the presence of PS. The insufficient CDs and their excess addition made the photoactivity of CDs@CuVs deteriorate. This control experiment verified the vital roles of CDs played in the improvement of photocatalytic performance for CDs@CuVs. Moreover, the removal efficiency of TC is positively related with the dosage of CDs@CuVs (Figure S10a). The effects of PS amounts on the removal efficiency of TC are nonlinear at the given dosage of photocatalysts (Figure S10b), indicative of a proper amount of PS required for catalytic activity and cost effectiveness. In consideration with the coexistence of various anions such as Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and HCO3 −, which often weaken the catalytic activities of PS by reacting with active radicals generated, we further explored the effect of these anions on TC oxidation in the CDs@CuVs/PS system.

From Fig. 3c-e, the concomitant Cl−, SO4 2− and NO3 − exert an insignificant effect on TC removal. Although these anions could react with free radicals such as SO4
− and OH to change their type and concentration, the produced reactive radicals of Cl and NO3 still keep high redox potentials (2.4 V and 2.3 ∼ 2.5 V for Cl and NO3 , respectively) [43], which are slightly lower than hydroxyl radicals. Oxidation reaction of TC remains and thereby inhibition on its degradation is unnoticeable from experiments. Nevertheless, the oxygen atom in NO3 can fill in OVs to form NO2 radicals [44], and so impede the adsorption of PS at OVs and make PS activation weaken to influence photodegradation efficiency of TC. The HCO3 − beyond the critical concentration has a clear inhibitory effect, showing the decrease in TC removal from 100 % to 90 % and 87 % in the coexistence of 5 and 10 mM of HCO3 −, respectively (Fig. 3f). By reacting with OH, analogously, bicarbonate/carbonate ions can transform into HCO3 /CO3
− radicals, which exhibit significantly lower redox potential (1.59 V) and reaction rate with TC than OH species [43]. Thus, they play a detrimental role in the degradation of OH-induced organic pollutants. What’s more, we evaluated the impact of metal cations including Ca2+, Na+ and K+ and found their negligible effect on TC degradation (Figure S11).

CDs@CuVs were recycled four times (Fig. 4 a) to illustrate the reusability and stability of the photocatalyst. The sample still accomplished a ∼ 90 % degradation of TC after repetitively used for four times. An insignificant recession in photocatalytic performance could be caused by a small amount of catalyst losswhile washing. The well-retained crystallinity and chemical structure after catalysis also reflect the excellent stability and durability of CDs@CuVs. By comparison with XRD patterns of the sample before and after reaction (Fig. 4b), we did not observe the visible changes of diffraction peaks. What’s more important, we found more amounts of Cu+ and OVs in the reused sample than original one by XPS and EPR. The content of Cu+ in CDs@CuVs increases from 31.7 % to 43.8 % after catalytic reaction (Fig. 4c). This could derive from the delocalized electrons in CDs, which endow them with a wide range of redox states and reversible redox activity. Under light excitation, a part of delocalized electrons in CDs transfers to CuVs and behaves with reduction action. Therefore, some of divalent coppers in CuVs are transformed into monovalent coppers, leading to the formation of new OVs. XPS O 1 s spectra demonstrate the enhanced proportion of OVs from 32.5 % to 46.8 % after catalytic reaction of CDs@CuVs (Figure S12). The corresponding EPR signal at g = 2.003 becomes stronger as well (Fig. 4d).

To clarify activation process of PS, we used chemical scavengers to identify possible active species and their roles in TC degradation [5,45]. From Fig. 5 a, 200 mM of MeOH as a scavenger with high response to both SO4
− (9.7 × 108 M−1·s−1) and OH (2.5 × 107 M−1·s−1) [5] was added into photocatalytic reaction and enabled the TC degradation efficiency to decrease by 42 %. Furthermore, MeOH was replaced by another scavenger of TBA to detect OH [5]. TC degradation efficiency reduces by 38 %. Accordingly, the OH radicals could play more important roles in TC oxidation. To confirm it, EPR was performed and demonstrated that the generated amount of SO4
− is far lower than that of OH (Fig. 5b and Figure S13). We further used benzoquinone (BQ) to scavenge O2 ▪− and found no any effect on TC degradation rate [45,46]. Then we added FFA and TEOA to photocatalytic reaction to determine the roles of 1O2 and h+ in TC decomposition, respectively [5,47]. The significant inhibitory effects were observed. EPR results verified that mild O2 ▪− and abundant 1O2 were present in the photocatalytic reaction system (Fig. 5c, d). As thus, both 1O2 and h+ are crucial active species for TC oxidation. On the other side, the generated amount of OH radicals from CDs@CuVs was markedly higher than that of CuVs. Unexpectedly, EPR revealed the similar 1O2 amounts formed by both photocatalysts, inconsistent with their huge differences in photocatalytic degradation performance of TC. To unveil the reason, we further checked the 1O2 amounts produced from both samples in the absence of PS under light irradiation (Figure S14). Instead, CDs@CuVs generated remarkably more 1O2 than CuVs. So we proposed the following photocatalytic processes as illustrated in Fig. 6 a.

Upon proper photon excitation, the generated charges of h+ in the valance band (VB) and e− in the conduction band (CB) of photocatalysts after separation are able to diffuse toward their surfaces. In general, the migration rate of e− in CB is kinetically faster than that of h+ in VB. Thus, the e− could be preferentially captured by OVs as the charge trapping centers. The electron energy levels of CB for CuVs and CDs@CuVs are insufficient to reduce O2 by a single e− reduction (−0.33 V for O2/O2
−) based on the diagram of the band energy potentials obtained by XPS valence band spectra and Tauc plots (Figure S15). However, OVs as active sites may fulfill the reduction reaction of O2 to produce O2 ▪− via strongly absorbed effects [33], and result in the 1O2 formation by the further reaction of O2 ▪− + h→1O2. Compared to CuVs, CDs@CuVs possessed not only more OVs but also more superior charge separation efficiency due to the presence of IEF. Accordingly, the generated amount of 1O2 from CDs@CuVs was more than that from CuVs.

When PS was added in photocatalytic reaction system, OVs as active sites of photocatalysts would interact with PS to trigger the reactions (1)–(3). On the one hand, the transferred e− was consumed by the reaction (1) and reduced the amount of O2 ▪− as produced. Thus, the kinetically slower h+ cannot meet enough reactant O2 ▪− to yield 1O2 and the superfluous h+ would involve in oxidization reaction of TC as the active species. On the other hand, the Cu+ in OVs had an ability to directly activate PS by the reaction (2). Similarly, the e− consumption by reaction (3) would diminish 1O2 outputs. Notably, the generated SO4
− radicals from reactions (1) and (2) could be fleetly transformed into OH radicals by the reaction (4). Consequently, the excited e− upon photocatalysts played an important role in PS activation to yield more OH free radicals. In combination with the scavenged experiments of active species shown in Fig. 5a, TC degradation efficiency was determined by the active species of 1O2, h+ and OH. Therefore, we here proposed that TC oxidation could be initiated by the endoperoxide formation by the reaction of non-radical 1O2 with TC. Both h+ and OH radicals subsequently attack to TC endoperoxide and further induce continuous oxidation reaction. Since the decomposition of TC is completed by the cooperation of 1O2 with h+ and OH, its removal efficiency declines sharply if one of 1O2, h+ and OH is scavenged.
(1) S2O8 2− + e− → SO4
− + SO4 2−
(2) S2O8 2− + Cu+ → SO4
− + SO4 2− + Cu2+
(3) Cu2+ + e− → Cu+
(4) SO4
− + H2O → OH + SO4 2− + H+

Fig. 6b provides the amperometric i-t curve of CDs@CuVs in response to the addition of PS and TC. Under visible light irradiation, CDs@CuVs can output a stable current with irradiation time. After adding PS, the photocurrent steeply elevates firstly and shortly restores afterwards, revealing the instantaneous transfer of photogenerated charges from CDs@CuVs to PS for activation reaction [48]. A similar phenomenon occurs after adding TC. Therefore, the separated carriers can directly involve in the whole catalytic reaction. Besides, we conducted in situ FT-IR spectrometry to determine the intermediate species during the photocatalytic reaction process (Fig. 6c). The fresh vibration peaks at around 1017 cm−1 and in the range of 3300–3500 cm−1can be assigned to the formation of OH radicals and C-OH/–OH during the photocatalytic process, respectively. The emerging bands at 1400–1450 cm−1 are ascribed to the formation of –NO2, resulting from the oxidation of N-contained groups in TC. What’s more, the signals of CO2 appear at 2300 cm−1 [49], confirming the oxidative decomposition of TC under light irradiation.

We have demonstrated an efficient strategy to optimize OVs and photoinduced charge separation properties in photocatalysts for PS activation. By rationally incorporating low-cost and green CDs into inorganic semiconductor CuVs through a simple two-step hydrothermal method, we found that CDs can not only induce the formation of more OVs as active sites, but also facilitate the charge separation and transfer by means of interface-induced IEF under visible light irradiation. This allows photocatalysts with more available carriers and active sites to involve in catalytic reaction. Utilizing this strategy, our presented CDs@CuVs showed the expected photocatalytic capacity to activate PS in water and enabled TC degradation efficiency approximately 8 times higher than the CDs-free CuVs under visible light irradiation. It turns out that the decomposition of TC is fulfilled by the active species cooperation of 1O2 with h+ and OH. Accordingly, this work paves an avenue to photocatalyst design for PS activation using low-cost and eco-friendly CDs.

Ernan Pang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shijia Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jie Wu: Resources, Funding acquisition. Qing Chang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Data curation. Ning Li: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shengliang Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is jointly supported by the special fund for Science and Technology Innovation Teams of Shanxi Province (202304051001014), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022-136) and Project Selection and Priority Funding for Scientific and Technological Activities of Returned Overseas Chinese (20240012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.12.065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
